text: The surgeon ensures the cut edge of the ileum mesentery is straight, aligning the ileum next to the transverse colon. The robotic third arm provides counter traction on the bowel. An enterotomy is made on the anti-mesenteric side of the bowel using cautery, with the surgeon lifting the tissue to achieve a full-thickness incision into the intestinal lumen.

The surgeon uses the robotic third arm to provide counter-traction on the transverse colon, avoiding the tinea coli for a more anti-mesenteric position. The colon is opened sharply with cautery, similar to the previous bowel opening. Scissors are used with one blade inside and one blade outside the bowel wall, lifting and cutting. The stapler is inserted into the ilium first, demonstrating the ease of positioning the mobile ilium relative to the immobile transverse colon.

The surgeon positions the ileum and brings the stapler into the colon, achieving a precise alignment. The stapler is fired, and the third arm is used to retract the apex of the staple line upwards, ensuring the edges are aligned. The staple line is inspected, showing no bleeding and a well-exposed crotch. The surgeon begins closing the bottom crotch with a 3-0 V-lock PDS suture on a V-twin needle, taking smaller bites for secure closure. A defective needle is replaced with a new one to continue suturing.

The surgeon uses the third arm to retract the apex of the staple line, aligning the edges of the bowel efficiently. Full thickness bites are taken along the suture line, exposing the apex clearly. The surgeon retracts the bowel posterior to the apex, rolling the tip in with large bites to complete the closure. The suture is run from the apex down, taking seromuscular bites on both sides of the suture line to imbricate it in a pseudo-limber fashion.

The surgeon uses a long, twelve-inch relock suture to roll the suture line over itself, providing an extra layer of protection. The edges of the bowel visibly roll in on themselves as the surgeon works down to the bottom crotch of the staple line, taking thick, full-thickness bites to secure the closure. A crotch stitch is placed at the point of maximal tension to prevent the staple line from unrolling, resulting in a completed anastomosis.
